articles.title: Cheap Text, Expensive Claims: Fee Asymmetry and the Growing Patent Document
articles.content:
丹尼斯·克劳奇

2008年，我发表了一项简短的实证研究，表明美国专利在各个维度上都在增长。说明书越来越长，权利要求越来越多，两种趋势同步发展。故事很简单：专利正在变大。

近二十年后，情况已一分为二。我的新文章《不断扩展的专利文件：更少的权利要求、更多的文字以及增长的驱动因素》发表于《国际知识产权法杂志》第33卷第371页（2026年），该文基于已公布的实用专利申请及其授权专利的全部数据。四十年来，说明书增长了近四倍。相比之下，权利要求数量在2005年左右达到峰值，此后持续下降。专利文件在一个方向上扩展，却在另一个方向上收缩。

我在文章中的核心论点是，这种分歧反映的是供给侧经济学，而非专利 doctrine 需求侧的响应。领先的需求侧候选因素是Alice Corp. Pty. Ltd. v. CLS Bank International案（573 U.S. 208，2014年），该案收紧了软件和商业方法的 eligibility 要求，本应预期会推动申请人提供更详细的技术披露。但数据显示并无此效应。说明书增长在过去二十年平滑且单调，在Alice案、《美国发明法案》或任何其他 doctrine 事件时均无结构性断裂。增长也与USPTO授权率无关，该授权率在研究期间波动了近三十个百分点。Alice案改变了审查员评估申请的方式，但并未改变说明书的篇幅。
articles.summary:
by Dennis Crouch

In 2008, I published a short empirical study showing that U.S. patents were growing in every dimension. Specifications were getting longer and claims were getting more numerous, and the two trends moved together. The story was simple: patents were getting bigger.

Nearly two decades later, the picture has split in two. My new article, The Expanding Patent Document: Fewer Claims, More Words, and the Drivers of Growth, 33 J. Intell. Prop. L. 371 (2026), draws on the full population of published utility applications and their issued patents. Specifications have nearly quadrupled over four decades. Claim counts, by contrast, peaked around 2005 and have declined ever since. The patent document is expanding in one direction while contracting in the other.

My central claim in the article is that this divergence reflects supply-side economics rather than any demand-side response to patent doctrine. The leading demand-side candidate is Alice Corp. Pty. Ltd. v. CLS Bank International, 573 U.S. 208 (2014), which tightened eligibility for software and business methods and might have been expected to push applicants toward more detailed technical disclosure. The data show no such effect. Specification growth is smooth and monotonic across two decades, with no structural break at Alice, at the America Invents Act, or at any other doctrinal event. Growth is also uncorrelated with the USPTO allowance rate, which swung nearly thirty percentage points over the study period. Alice changed how examiners evaluated applications. It did not change how long specifications are.

articles.title: Plain, Ordinary, and Unresolved: Woodway’s Two-Front Claim Construction Fight
articles.content:
作者：丹尼斯·克劳特（Dennis Crouch）

在我看来，几乎每个专利案件都涉及权利要求解释程序，这似乎有些疯狂。这些权利要求本就由专业的专利律师起草，并经历了严格的审查程序，旨在消除任何不确定性或模糊性。但当我看到法院对其他文本的解释时，心情就平静了一些。法院通常会在面对新颁布的法规或合同解释的复杂性时，经历其“初次印象”的艰难过程。

在诉讼中，通常是被控侵权人寻求特定的解释——要么是狭义解释以避免侵权，要么是广义解释以触发无效宣告。更确切地说，这一解释过程将案件交到法官手中，而被告希望法官能在案件提交陪审团之前就予以驳回。专利权人则更常主张“通常和惯常含义”——这是“信任陪审团”的代名词，意即让陪审团听取证据，包括本领域技术人员的理解。

权利要求解释本身是一个中间步骤，是一个初步的法律问题，旨在提出真正的争议点，而非直接解决它们。实际上，这一步骤往往决定了整个案件的走向。侵权和有效性是事实认定者（陪审团）需要判断的问题，但前提是案件能进入陪审团审理阶段，而权利要求解释可能阻止案件进入这一阶段。这里的一个风险是过度解释。法院被要求解决关于某个术语含义的真实争议时，可能会滑向决定该术语如何适用于被控设备，或是否被现有技术所公开，从而撰写定义，使权利要求涵盖或不涵盖相关产品或参考文献。这不再是解释权利要求，而是在决定侵权或无效，却将其称为解释。

= = =

本周，我收听了伍德韦诉莱弗科雷健身设备公司（袭击）案的口头辩论。这是一个相当简单的案例，我希望得到一份写得很好的具有判例效力的判决书，可以用于教学——我经常使用体育器材专利案例，因为它们在物理上可演示，通常也直观易懂。

这次上诉涉及两个并行审理的案件——专利权人伍德韦最初在PTAB和地方法院都败诉了：

这两起争讼都涉及同一产品线，即伍德韦公司手动驱动的CURVE跑步机，以及被控侵权的袭击气动跑步机（如下图所示）。
articles.summary:
by Dennis Crouch

It seems a bit crazy to me that almost every patent case involves a process of claim construction.  Those claims were already drafted by skilled patent attorneys and survived a rigorous examination process designed to wring-out  any uncertainty or ambiguity.  But, I calm down a bit when I look at court interpretation of other texts.  Courts typically struggle through their "first impression" of a newly enacted statute, not to mention the vagaries of contract interpretation.

In litigation, it is often the accused infringer seeking a specific construction - either a narrow construction to avoid infringement or a broad construction to trigger invalidity.  More to point, that construction process puts the case in the hands of the judge who - the defendant hopes - will dismiss the case before it even reaches the jury.  Patentees more often push for "plain and ordinary meaning" -- this is code for "trust the jury" to receive the evidence, including understanding of a skilled artisan.

Claim construction is itself an intermediary step, a preliminary question of law that is supposed to tee up the real issues rather than resolve them. In practice it is frequently the step that decides everything. Infringement and validity are factfinder (jury) questions, but only if they reach the jury, and a construction can keep them from getting there. One hazard here is over-construction. A court asked to settle a genuine dispute about what a term means can slide into deciding how that term applies to the accused device or whether it is taught by the prior art, writing the definition so that the claim does, or does not, extend to relevant product or reference. That is no longer construing the claim. It is deciding infringement or invalidity and calling it construction.

= = =

This week, I tuned in to oral arguments in Woodway v. LifeCORE Fitness (Assault).  This is a pretty simple case and I'm hoping for a well written precedential opinion that I can use for teaching -- I often use sports equipment patent examples because they are physically demonstrable and typically intuitively understandable.

The appeal involves two cases that have been running in parallel -- with the patentee Woodway originally losing in both the PTAB and district court:

  • 2025-1323 comes from an inter partes review and turns on whether the prior art discloses a "curved running surface."
  • 2025-1431, comes from the Southern District of California and turns on the meaning of "substantially prevents" in Woodway's curved-treadmill patent family.

Both fights concern the same product line, Woodway's manually powered CURVE treadmill, and the accused Assault AirRunner (Shown below).

To continue reading, become a Patently-O member. Already a member? Simply log in to access the full post.
